# Chapter 05

[gxxtdtmh] The rat-rod didn’t sleep. It waited under its tarp like a dog with one eye open, smelling the morning. Flynn woke to the generator’s last cough and the thin hush that followed, the Compound briefly holding its breath before Diesel brought it back to life.

[ntaohgvb] Dawn was the only time the desert felt merciful. The heat hadn’t climbed yet. The light was pink and thin and made every piece of metal look clean. Flynn walked out from the trailer with his hands in his pockets, trying not to look like he was looking for Cage.

[xwijdkjh] Cage was already there, leaning on the rat-rod’s fender with a cigarette unlit between his fingers. He watched the eastern sky as if it owed him something. “You ready,” he said, not as a question.

[o1hz0j3k] Flynn nodded. His mouth was dry. His implant stayed hard-off and the silence in his skull made room for other things—fear, excitement, the small animal part of him that wanted to run.

[8tr3kij0] Diesel came out from behind the station roof with a mug and a wrench. She didn’t give Flynn the wrench this time. She gave him a look that said: *don’t make me scrape you off sand.* “Wash,” she said. “Then maybe the service road. Not the highway.”

[2ijzlhai] Cage smiled like he’d already agreed to something else. “He’s not stupid,” he said.

[5rtlbmzn] “He’s new,” Diesel replied. “That’s the same thing for now.”

[9lansirn] Don and Wrench stood a little apart, half awake. Don’s arms were crossed against the cold. Wrench held coffee in a chipped mug and watched Flynn like he was watching a fuse burn.

[ixg2ncam] Birdie was up on the roof again, legs dangling, a radio headset around his neck like a toy soldier. He called down, “If you crash I get your shoes.”

[izh0as0z] Flynn tried to laugh and it came out thin.

[dxrvus9t] Cage pulled the tarp off. The rat-rod’s metal caught the dawn and threw it back in dull flashes. The car was ugly and honest, every weld visible, every bolt a confession. Cage slapped the roof. “She’ll teach you,” he said.

[skqfkqsb] Flynn climbed in. The seat belt bit his shoulder. The steering wheel felt alive even with the engine off, vibrating with wind and nervous expectation.

[gdxgpzef] Cage settled into the passenger seat with his knee angled toward the dash like he owned the space. He didn’t buckle until Diesel tapped the frame with her wrench. He did it then, slow, eyes on Diesel the whole time.

[bq8v2omi] Diesel leaned in through the open window. “You stall, you don’t panic,” she said to Flynn. “You slide, you don’t overcorrect. You hear something wrong, you stop. You don’t impress him.” She jerked her chin toward Cage without looking at him.

[lroqzx4b] Cage’s grin widened. “He should impress me,” he said.

[bsm4yvr2] Diesel’s gaze finally cut to him. “He should live,” she said, and stepped back.

[zmb7x2x7] Cage turned the key. The engine coughed, caught, and settled into that uneven, hungry idle. Flynn’s hands tightened on the wheel until his knuckles went pale.

[nmeuaoku] “All right,” Cage said. “Clutch in. First gear. Give her a whisper of gas. Find the bite. Same as yesterday. Just… more.”

[pmwb46jb] Flynn eased out of the Compound and into the wash, tires crunching over stones. The car wobbled on its suspension like it was balancing on bones. The steering had slack and then sudden bite, like a lie turning into truth.

[d1hvhyiz] He got to twenty, then twenty-five. The wind found every gap in the metal and whistled. Sand flicked up against the floorboards. The engine note rose and Flynn felt the vibration in his teeth.

[hiy0ovxv] He wanted to look at Cage, to see if Cage was watching him. He kept his eyes forward instead because the wash was a corridor of small mistakes waiting to happen.

[ztvthhwi] “Good,” Cage said, as if he’d heard Flynn’s thoughts. “Now give it a little more. Don’t baby it. Cars hate fear.”

[cq9szvra] Flynn pressed the gas. The rat-rod surged. The rear end stepped out a fraction on loose sand and Flynn corrected, hands moving before his brain had time to argue.

[30nlmtrs] He laughed, surprised. The sound came from somewhere deep, like it had been buried and dug up.

[xfdt1rpe] Cage’s laughter joined his, softer. “There,” he said. “That’s you. Not the city version.”

[t6rread0] They reached the far end of the wash where it met a cracked service road. The road ran straight toward the solar farms, then bent away, skirting their edge like a body avoiding a knife.

[ijwxawjj] Diesel’s voice came through the handheld radio clipped to Cage’s belt. “Stay off the grid roads,” she said. “No dumb hero stuff.”

[k9hlkzfu] Cage picked up the radio and held it near his mouth without pressing the talk button. “She loves us,” he said.

[l2oosbwt] Flynn’s stomach tightened again. The service road looked smooth compared to the wash. Smooth meant speed.

[7uincfa2] Cage finally pressed the button. “Copy,” he said, polite as a clerk.

[elr68hsi] They rolled onto the road. The tires hummed. The engine smoothed out and the car stopped feeling like it would fall apart at any second. That made it worse.

[pae83d8w] Flynn pushed to forty. Then fifty. The wind became a steady roar. The desert on either side blurred into pale streaks.

[yiojbh3c] At sixty, the rat-rod started to feel like it wanted to fly apart. The hood vibrated. The steering wheel trembled under Flynn’s palms.

[7hkk1vpk] Cage leaned back and closed his eyes for a moment, like a man listening to music.

[viigkuda] “You can’t close your eyes,” Flynn said, half joking.

[ukpw0yry] Cage opened them. “I can,” he said. “I know the road.”

[mteifxc0] Flynn’s mouth went dry again. He kept his eyes on the asphalt. The service road was empty except for windblown trash and a strip of rubber that might have been a tire or might have been a snake.

[agc4n7o8] “Eighty,” Cage said.

[icneq8yi] “What?” Flynn asked.

[r7wayxwj] “Take it to eighty,” Cage said, calm. “If you don’t, you’ll always think you could’ve. If you do, you’ll learn what the car tells you at eighty.”

[ewpy1mxw] Flynn swallowed. He pushed. The needle climbed. Seventy. Seventy-five. The engine’s roar turned into a scream that made the bones behind his ears ache.

[raa7swrh] At eighty, the world narrowed. The road became a single line. Flynn’s breathing went shallow. His hands were light on the wheel because if he gripped too hard he’d jerk it and die.

[oyipkbz2] Cage’s voice stayed low. “Don’t stare at the end,” he said. “Look where you want to be next. The car goes where your eyes go.”

[mrrsemcr] Flynn forced himself to look farther ahead, then farther still. The distance shimmered. The solar farms to the left flashed with light like scales.

[dvd68srl] A shape appeared in the road ahead. Not big. Just wrong. A dark lump half in the lane.

[sqoqphtz] Flynn’s brain named it: *debris.* His body wanted to slam the brakes.

[neveidln] “Don’t,” Cage said.

[caorlkpm] Flynn’s foot hovered, trembling. He remembered Diesel’s words: don’t freeze. He remembered Cage’s: cars hate fear. He remembered his own laugh in the wash.

[poboy9yp] He eased off the gas instead of stabbing the brake. He held the wheel with two fingers and nudged the car left, not much, just enough.

[xfwtkkw1] The rat-rod skimmed past the debris close enough that Flynn felt air slap the side panel. It was a shredded tire carcass, cords flailing like hair.

[m0ovai1h] The car wiggled. Flynn corrected. The tires found grip again and the line held.

[rovlf5ml] For half a second Flynn saw a different future: the car spinning, Cage laughing, Diesel’s face when they didn’t come back. The future disappeared as quickly as it had come.

[fierj3mh] Flynn let out a sound that was half sob and half laugh. His eyes stung.

[yt05wq8r] Cage clapped once, sharp, and the sound startled Flynn more than the debris had. “Good,” Cage said. “You didn’t flinch. You didn’t beg the car to save you. You drove it.”

[cbo5whmg] Flynn’s hands were shaking now. Not from fear exactly. From his body trying to return to the size it had been before the road narrowed. He kept the car steady on muscle memory while his mind replayed the tire carcass like a coin he couldn’t stop flipping.

[luquoeq5] Cage leaned forward and put two fingers on the dash like he was touching a pulse. “Hear that?” he asked.

[hhnydimy] “The engine?” Flynn said.

[lyoijp8h] “The engine, the tires, the wind,” Cage said. “They’re all telling you the same thing. Where you are. What you can get away with. You learn to listen and the car will warn you before the road does.”

[b0pbh04c] Flynn swallowed. He wanted to say he’d never listened to anything like that in his life. In the city, the only warning you got was a polite chime and a suggestion to reroute.

[rttz8of4] Cage sat back and watched the solar farms slide by at the edge of vision. “They built all that and called it a future,” he said. “But they still can’t make you feel alive. Not without taking your hands.”

[iwijidcl] Flynn didn’t trust himself to answer. He kept both hands on the wheel, light, feeling the tremor in the steering column and the little changes in resistance as the surface shifted.

[n4oc9hpt] Cage pointed ahead where the service road bent away from the panels. “Take the curve clean,” he said. “No hero move. Just clean.”

[pn121qr8] Flynn eased off the gas before the bend. The speed dropped into the seventies. The car settled. He turned in and held a steady arc, not fighting, not forcing.

[m5nth9ai] For a moment it felt smooth. Almost civilized. The danger hid itself. That was how it got you.

[4u6ijyjg] Cage nodded like a judge. “Again,” he said. “Same bend. Same line. Until it’s boring.”

[mhixikh4] “Boring?” Flynn managed.

[gjyr5nt7] Cage’s smile flashed. “If it’s not boring you’re not in control,” he said. “Control is boring. Then you add speed on purpose.”

[tlwytec5] They looped the bend twice more. Each pass made Flynn’s fear thinner, more manageable, like something you could fold and put in a pocket instead of something that filled the whole world.

[lfu2tfsd] On the third pass the rat-rod’s rear stepped out a hair and Flynn corrected, smooth. No panic. No overcorrection. Just a small conversation between his hands and the car.

[qcxlx8my] Cage let out a pleased breath. “There,” he said. “That’s driving. Not running.”

[axmryq58] Flynn felt a hot prickle behind his eyes and hated it. He wasn’t a crying person. But something in him had been waiting years for permission to be this present.

[6souhize] “Okay,” Cage said at last. “Bring it down. We go home with the same car we left with.”

[5jscuq1f] Flynn’s throat worked. “I almost—”

[kfj3ii7q] “Everybody almost,” Cage said. “That’s the point.”

[fwwyxivz] Diesel’s voice cracked over the radio again. “Status,” she said.

[ambnbyxd] Cage pressed the button. “Fine,” he said. “He’s fine.”

[tfkqpfhi] Flynn realized Cage hadn’t said *we.* He hadn’t said *the car.* He’d said *he.* Like Flynn was the only thing that mattered in this experiment.

[ihxocwds] Cage clicked the radio off and looked at Flynn with something that might have been approval or might have been hunger. “This is the only honest thing left,” he said. “You and the machine and the consequences. No coupons. No pop-ups. No one rescuing you from your own hands.”

[1ssyer4b] Flynn’s heart hammered. He felt the truth of it in his bones even while another part of him heard the danger in the way Cage said *honest*.

[xxdgcr6r] Cage leaned closer, voice quieter. “You see why people follow me,” he said.

[veyuiomt] Flynn didn’t answer. He couldn’t tell if saying yes would make him a believer or a liar.

[2xragbrh] Cage sat back and smiled like he’d heard the answer anyway. “Bring it down,” he said. “Let her cool. We don’t need a rod through the block.”

[gx8ruvjx] Flynn eased off the gas. The speed fell in tens. The world widened again. His lungs filled properly. His hands started shaking now that the danger had passed, as if his body had been saving the tremor for later.

[c2ufd4so] When they rolled back into the wash and then into the Compound, Diesel was waiting with her arms crossed, mug forgotten on a crate.

[09znzhin] Flynn killed the engine and the silence hit him like a wave. He climbed out on weak legs. Sand stuck to his shoes. He felt taller and smaller at the same time.

[vbkomjii] Diesel walked up and took his face in her hands, thumbs at his jaw like she was checking for fractures. She looked him in the eyes. “You okay,” she said.

[0rswdzgk] Flynn nodded, too fast.

[6vpyhmff] Diesel looked past him at Cage. “You push him?”

[pw0xlbmt] Cage smiled. “I taught him,” he said.

[m2lyoglr] Diesel’s eyes narrowed. “Teaching isn’t pushing,” she said.

[gy1mksft] Cage’s smile didn’t move. “Same road,” he said.

[ofdkqmlw] Flynn heard the words and understood nothing and everything at once.

[pm44rzyl] That night by the fire he couldn’t eat. He sat with his hands wrapped around an enamel mug of water warmed by the flames. The pods on the highway slid past in their safe silence. Flynn listened to the distant hush and felt the memory of eighty miles an hour in his teeth.

[1bclnj4c] Diesel sat across from him sharpening a blade with a stone, slow strokes, eyes on the dark beyond the trailers. The sound was steady and intimate, like breathing. Flynn watched her hands and wondered how many times those hands had kept this place from exploding.

[byztkekz] “You did good,” Diesel said without looking up.

[tnn4ebxm] Flynn’s throat tightened again. Praise from Diesel felt different than Cage’s. Diesel’s praise didn’t want anything from him.

[xrsmfkhm] “He pushed you,” Diesel added.

[n9lrrjgf] Flynn opened his mouth, then closed it. He could picture Cage’s smile. He could picture the tire carcass. He could picture his own hands moving without permission. “He taught me,” Flynn said, and heard how thin it sounded.

[oulvmwhq] Diesel’s stone kept moving. “Sometimes teaching is pushing,” she said. “Just keep your eyes open. That’s all I’m asking.”

[6jwq3fu0] Birdie told a story about a pod that had tried to arrest itself after running over a coyote. Don laughed once, tired. Wrench stared into the coals like he was watching the line between two lives.

[kcpkddti] Flynn waited for his implant to nag him about hydration or heart rate. It didn’t. The silence stayed. In the silence he realized something simple and terrifying.

[c5rb0xuv] He didn’t want the city back.
